THOUGHT:
The existing citations effectively cover ML engineering debt (Sculley et al., 2015), in-the-wild distribution shifts (Koh et al., 2020), and shortcut learning/spurious correlations (Geirhos et al., 2020). However, to fully address the required citation categories—particularly highlighting research gaps in benchmark evaluation, comparing offline vs. real-world findings, and supporting arguments about evaluation methodology pitfalls—I need a paper that critically examines the reliability of standard benchmark test sets. Recht et al. (2019) "Do CIFAR-10 Classifiers Generalize to CIFAR-10?" is a pivotal study that exposes how dataset contamination, labeling inconsistencies, and evaluation leakage create an illusion of progress. Citing this work will ground discussions on benchmark fragility, motivate independent test set construction, and bridge the gap between controlled academic evaluations and practical deployment reliability.
